paper_title: An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Agent evaluation requires assessing complex multi-step behaviors involving tool use and intermediate reasoning, making it costly and expertise-intensive. A natural question arises: can frontier coding assistants reliably automate this evaluation process? Our study shows that simply prompting coding assistants is insufficient for this task. Without domain-specific evaluation knowledge, frontier coding assistants achieve only a 30% execution success rate and produce over-engineered evaluations averaging 12+ metrics per agent, indicating that strong coding ability does not automatically translate to reliable agent evaluation. We introduce EvalAgent, an AI assistant that automates the end-to-end agent evaluation pipeline. EvalAgent encodes evaluation domain expertise as evaluation skills (procedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamically retrieved API documentation) that compose into a trace-based pipeline producing complete evaluation artifacts including metrics, executable code, and reports. To systematically assess generated evaluations, we introduce a meta-evaluation framework alongside AgentEvalBench, a benchmark comprising 20 agents, each paired with evaluation requirements and test scenarios. We further propose the Eval@1 metric to measure whether generated evaluation code both executes and yields meaningful results on the first run. Our experiments show that EvalAgent produces focused evaluations, improving Eval@1 from 17.5% to 65%, and achieving 79.5% human expert preference over baseline approaches. Further ablation studies show that evaluation skills are critical for handling complex evaluation: removing them causes Eval@1 to drop significantly from 65% to 30%.

sections.title: An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation

Repo: https://github.com/awslabs/Agent-EvalKit 1. Introduction Evaluation is the bottleneck in agent deployment. Autonomous AI agents now operate in high-stakes applications (Wang et al., 2023; Xi et al., 2023), such as code generation (Wang et al., 2024b), scientific discovery (Zhang et al., 2025b), and financial analysis (Han et al., 2024). Yet the tools to assess their quality lag far behind the tools to build them. The core difficulty is that agent evaluation differs fundamentally from LLM evaluation. While LLM evaluation focuses on output quality for single-turn or multi-turn conversations (Liu et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023), agent evaluation must assess execution traces containing sequences of reasoning steps, tool invocations, error recovery actions, and state transitions that unfold over time (He et al., 2025; Kim et al., 2025). Only evaluating the final output is insufficient because (1) correct final answer may mask flawed reasoning (Lightman et al., 2023) and (2) failed output may not reflect robust error handling. These behavioral nuances are invisible to output-only methods and are precisely what production deployments need to assess. Equal contribution Corresponding author: hbding@amazon.com An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Agent evaluation also demands significant domain expertise and imposes practical overhead. Practitioners must carefully design evaluation criteria tailored to diverse agent behaviors, instrument agent code to capture relevant signals, and manually inspect execution traces to diagnose failures (Mohammadi et al., 2025; Yehudai et al., 2025). common workaround is to embed evaluation logic directly within agent code (Confident-AI, 2024; Rawert et al., 2023). However, this tightly couples evaluation with implementation, causing evaluation behavior to diverge between development and production environments. These challenges point to clear need: automating the agent evaluation process itself. Prior work on LLM-as-a-judge (Liu et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023) and agentic judge (Chen et al., 2025; He et al., 2025; Kim et al., 2025; Zhuge et al., 2024) uses models to directly produce verdicts against pre-defined criteria. But judgment presupposes that criteria already exist. We identify harder problem: evaluation generation, given an agents source code and execution traces, automatically generating the evaluation criteria, executable assessment code, and actionable reports. This requires not just assessing quality, but designing what to assess and how, challenge that existing approaches leave unaddressed. Can frontier coding assistants (e.g., Claude Code (Anthropic, 2025b)) automate this process? Our study shows that they cannot do so reliably without intervention, exhibiting three systematic failure modes. First, metric proliferation: without evaluation-specific guidance, they generate 12+ scattered metrics per agent, favoring operational measurements like latency percentiles and token counts over meaningful assessments of task success. Second, code over-engineering: generated evaluation code is 23 longer than necessary, fragmented across 57 files with premature abstractions that undermine maintainability. Third, plan-code drift: they generate evaluation plans with well-reasoned semantic rubrics but implement shallow keyword-counting heuristics instead. These failures reflect fundamental mismatch between general code generation and the specialized reasoning that evaluation design demands. We present EvalAgent (2), system that transforms agent source code and user requirements into executable evaluation artifacts. EvalAgent encodes evaluation domain expertise as evaluation skills self-contained packages of procedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamically retrieved API documentation (Zhang et al., 2025a)that compose into trace-based pipeline: planning evaluation criteria, generating test scenarios, instrumenting the agent for trace collection, producing executable assessment code, and generating actionable reports. Each pipeline stage loads only the relevant skills on demand, channeling generation toward focused, implementable assessments rather than the scattered metrics and shallow heuristics produced by unconstrained approaches. key challenge remains: how do we know that EvalAgent produces good evaluations? To assess evaluation quality without ground truth, we introduce meta-evaluation framework (3) using pairwise comparison across five dimensions (requirement fulfillment, metric relevance, code quality, plan quality, and plan-code alignment) alongside AgentEvalBench, benchmark comprising 20 diverse agents spanning 9 frameworks and 14 application domains, each paired with 2 user requirements and 5 test scenarios. Expert annotators achieve Fleiss 𝜅 = 0.923 and prefer EvalAgent in 79.5% (10.5% ties) of comparisons. Across all conditions, EvalAgent achieves an 84% win-tie rate over baselines and 65.0% Eval@1the rate at which generated evaluations execute successfully and produce substantive results on the first attempt (4.1). Through design space ablations (4.2) and qualitative analysis (4.3), we observed: 1. Evaluation skills stabilize executability as metric count grows (65% Eval@1 at 5 metrics vs. 3040% for baselines), reliably steer generation away from shallow keyword matching toward meaningful semantic assessment, and keep generated code 2.46.6 more concise than baselines. 2 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Figure 1 EvalAgent pipeline. EvalAgent maps agent code and user requirements to an executable evaluation workspace and actionable report through six-stage process: (1) evaluation planning, (2) test case generation, (3) agent instrumentation, (4) trace collection and processing, (5) evaluation code generation and execution, and (6) reporting with actionable recommendations. Evaluation skills guide each stage. 2. Planning without evaluation domain knowledge hurts more than it helps. Directly generating evaluation code outperforms planning-then-implementing (74% win-tie). Unstructured planning expands scope rather than constraining it, producing 4.4 longer plans, 6.6 more code, and large amounts of unused files. Planning becomes effective when paired with evaluation skills. 3. EvalAgent is particularly effective under underspecified requirements. When users provide only generic instructions like evaluate this agent, EvalAgent achieves 95% win-tie rates over baselines by inferring meaningful metrics from observed behavior, compared to 80% when requirements are well-specified and simpler methods can partially compensate. 2. EvalAgent EvalAgent is an agentic system that transforms agent source code and user requirements into executable evaluation code and actionable reports through six-stage pipeline (Figure 1). General-purpose coding agents already possess the capabilities needed for evaluationcode understanding, test design, implementationbut lack evaluation-specific knowledge about what to measure and how. EvalAgent encodes this missing knowledge as evaluation skills (Zhang et al., 2025a): self-contained packages of instructions, executable code, and reference materials that the agent loads on demand at each pipeline stage. Skills use progressive disclosure, so only the relevant content is loaded at each stage, and skills are reused across stages. They fall into three categories (Appendix D.3 provides excerpts): 3 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Procedural instructions encode evaluation methodology as stage-specific workflows. Each skill defines step-by-step workflow, and constraints that scope the agents behavior. For instance, the planning skill instructs the agent to first analyze the agents architecture and trace behavior and identify metrics that each capture distinct behavioral aspect. The code generation skill prescribes workflow of implementing metrics, building main entry point, performing self-review, and updating dependencieswith explicit principles such as creating minimal working version first, validating library APIs before use, and following the evaluation plan exactly rather than expanding scope. Reusable code and templates provide implementation scaffolding shared across stages: plan template with sections for agent specification, metrics, and test scenarios; report template with executive summary and failure analysis structure; and executable code patterns for OTEL trace parsing and DeepEval (Confident-AI, 2024) metric integration. These reduce variance across runs and prevent the scope expansion observed in unconstrained approaches. Dynamic resources provide up-to-date external knowledge via Context7 (Upstash, 2024) for realtime API documentation retrieval. Library APIs evolve faster than model training cycles; Context7 fetches current signatures and usage patterns, preventing the deprecated-API errors that account for most executability failures in unconstrained systems (Section 4.2). These skills compose into six-stage pipeline, where each stage draws on specific combination of skills (Appendix provides full details): (1) Evaluation Planning. EvalAgent analyzes the agents source code and execution traces to produce structured evaluation plan: agent specification, focused metrics with scoring rubrics, and test scenarios. The planning prompt and plan template jointly guide the scope, preventing metric proliferation. (2) Test Case Generation. Test cases are generated in JSONL format, each specifying an input query, scenario category, and expected behavior. This phase is substitutable: any generator producing the expected schema can be used. (3) Agent Instrumentation. Lightweight OpenTelemetry (OpenTelemetry Community, 2026) instrumentation is added to the target agent via libraries such as Traceloop (Traceloop, 2026), requiring only few lines of code. (4) Trace Collection. The instrumented agent executes test cases, producing OTEL-compatible traces. trace processor filters to agent-relevant spans and extracts minimal field set: operation names, inputs/outputs, tool metadata, and timing. The trace extraction code patterns are reused in Phase 5. (5) Code Generation. EvalAgent translates the evaluation plan into executable Python code, generating metric implementations (both deterministic checks and LLM-based assessments), an evaluation orchestrator, and dependency manifest. This stage draws on all three skill categories: the code generation prompt enforces simplicity, code patterns provide trace parsing and metric scaffolding, and Context7 retrieves current API signatures. (6) Reporting. Results are synthesized into structured report following the report template: executive summary, per-metric analysis, failure root causes, and prioritized recommendations. 3. Meta-Evaluation and AgentEvalBench How should we evaluate an evaluator when no ground truth exists? We adopt comparative validity: determining which of two evaluation approaches is more useful under identical conditions, following construct validity principles from measurement theory (Cronbach and Meehl, 1955; Messick, 1995). 4 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Figure 2 Meta-evaluation framework. The agentic meta-evaluator performs pairwise comparisons between two approaches. (Left) Shared context and approach-specific artifacts. (Center) Evidence-grounded judgment across five weighted criteria with anti-length bias. (Right) Weighted aggregation yielding the final winner. We further introduce AgentEvalBench, benchmark for reproducible evaluation of agent evaluation systems. 3.1. Meta-Evaluation Framework Evaluating an evaluation system is inherently complex: it requires reading multi-file codebases, crossreferencing plans against implementations, and reasoning about whether metrics capture meaningful agent behavior. standard LLM-as-a-judge approach, which produces verdicts from single prompt, lacks the capacity for such multi-step inspection. We therefore employ an agentic meta-evaluator that judges evaluation artifacts through tool-augmented, multi-step reviewnavigating directories, reading plans, inspecting code, and examining traces. We adopt pairwise comparison following Thurstones law of comparative judgment (Thurstone, 1927), since relative judgments are more reliable and require less calibration than absolute scales (Wang et al., 2024a; Zheng et al., 2023). Figure 2 provides an overview. Dimensions. The meta-evaluator assesses quality across five dimensions  (Table 1)  . Dimension weights are set heuristically: Metric Relevance (MR) is highest (30%) because irrelevant metrics yield no actionable insight regardless of implementation quality; Code Quality & Complexity (CQC) follows (25%), as concise yet correct is necessary for practical adoption. The remaining weight is split equally among User Requirement Fulfillment (URF), Plan Quality (PQ), and Plan-Code Alignment (PCA) (15% each), which capture alignment with user intent and internal consistency. We explicitly enforce an anti-length bias: verbosity is not proxy for quality. When plans are unavailable (i.e., for methods without planning stage), weights renormalize to URF (25%), MR (40%), CQC (35%). 5 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Dimension Weight Description User Requirement Fulfillment (URF) Metric Relevance (MR) Code Quality & Complexity (CQC) Plan Quality (PQ) Plan-Code Alignment (PCA) 15% Which approach better addresses user requirements? 30% Which has higher signal-to-noise ratio in its metrics? 25% Which has more correct, organized, and maintainable code? 15% Which plan is more coherent, complete, and actionable? 15% Which implementation more faithfully follows its plan? Table 1 Meta-evaluation dimensions. Each dimension yields wins, wins, or Tie. When plans are unavailable, weights renormalize to URF (25%), MR (40%), CQC (35%). See Appendix for detailed rubrics. Scoring. For each dimension, the meta-evaluator assigns wins, wins, or Tie with evidence. Overall scores use weighted aggregation: Points𝐴 = 𝑑 𝑤𝑑 1[A wins 𝑑] + 0.5 𝑤𝑑 1[Tie on 𝑑]. (1) Our meta-evaluation focuses on plan construction and code synthesis using pre-collected traces to ensure controlled comparison. Evaluation success rate. Beyond qualitative meta-evaluation, we measure Eval@1 (evaluation success rate): the fraction of agents for which generated evaluation code both executes without errors and produces meaningful, non-vacuous results on the first run. Unlike the standard Pass@1 metric used in code generation (Chen et al., 2021), which only checks whether code runs, Eval@1 requires that the evaluation actually performs valid evaluation. For example, we count as failures evaluations that (i) run but produce uninformative outputs (e.g., constant or all-zero metrics), (ii) run on synthetic, mocked, or hardcoded data instead of real agent artifacts, or (iii) treat predicted values as the gold standard, which always gives the same score regardless of the agents behavior. 3.2. AgentEvalBench We introduce AgentEvalBench, to our knowledge the first benchmark for automated agent evaluation. It comprises 20 real-world agents, each paired with 5 test scenarios and 2 types of user requirements. Dataset. Agents were selected along three axes: (1) coverage of core capabilities (QA, RAG, tool use, multi-agent coordination), (2) representation of major frameworks alongside bespoke implementations, and (3) balanced complexity distribution across simple, medium, and hard tiers. Table 2 summarizes the full inventory. Agents expose up to 9 tools (mean 3.2), with traces ranging from 34 to 350+ events. Test scenarios. Each agent is paired with 5 test scenarios. For instance, career_assist scenarios cover job search, resume optimization, interview preparation, learning paths, and career transitions, while network_switch_operator_agent scenarios include device statistics queries and configuration tasks (see more examples in Appendix B.4). User requirements. Each agent has two types of evaluation requirements (see Appendix B.5 for examples). (i) Generic: Given the agent codebase and execution traces, create an evaluation code. This tests whether evaluation methods can infer appropriate metrics without domain guidance. (ii) Specific: e.g., evaluate correctness of extracted medical entities for medical_document_process or or precision and recall of recommendations for agent4rec_agent. 3.3. (i) Cross-model consistency tests whether different LLMs serving as meta-evaluators reach the same conclusions on identical evaluation pairs, ensuring that results are not an artifact of particular models biases. Using Claude Opus 4.5 and Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic, 2025a,b) as meta-evaluators yields 86.6% overall agreement, with both models preserving the same baseline ranking (Appendix H.1). (ii) Run-to-run consistency tests whether the same meta-evaluator produces stable judgments across independent runs, ruling out stochastic artifacts from sampling. Three independent runs of Claude Opus 4.5 produce 76.3% three-way agreement and 84.2% pairwise agreementwell above the 33.3% random baseline (Appendix H.2). 4. Experiments Setup. Our experiments evaluate the core challenges of automated agent evaluation: plan construction (what to measure) and code synthesis (correct implementation). All methods operate on the same set of pre-collected execution traces. We use 2 2 factorial design: two evaluator backbones (Claude Haiku 4.5, Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic, 2025b)) and two requirement types (generic, specific). The benchmark comprises 20 agents from AgentEvalBench. All meta-evaluations in the main paper use Claude Opus 4.5 (Anthropic, 2025a) as the meta-evaluator backbone; results with Sonnet 4.5 as meta-evaluator are provided in Appendix for robustness. EvalAgent is built on Claude Code v2.1.92, with Claude Sonnet 4.5 as its primary backbone, generates evaluation code integrating DeepEval (Confident-AI, 2024), and queries Context7 (Upstash, 2024) for up-to-date API documentation during code generation. Baselines. We compare against four baselines (see Table 4), each isolating specific design choices. LLM-Singleturn (B1) generates evaluation code in single prompt: agent code, traces, and requirements are embedded directly in the prompt, with no tool access or iterative refinement. The remaining baselines (B2, B3, B4) and EvalAgent are all built on top of coding assistant (Claude Code) with full tool accessfile reading, code editing, shell execution, and web searchand can perform multi-step reasoning over up to 50 tool calls. Agent-Sourcecode (B2) operates on source code only, without execution traces. Agent-Onestage (B3) adds trace access for direct code generation. Agent-Twostage (B4) introduces an explicit planning stage before implementation but lacks evaluation skills. EvalAgent (Ours) combines agentic reasoning, traces, planning, and evaluation skills. 4.1. EvalAgent achieves 84100% overall win-tie rates across all baselines and both evaluator LLMs (Table 5a). The most informative comparison is against B4, which shares the same two-stage architecture but lacks evaluation skills: EvalAgent achieves 87.590.0% overall win-tie, with particularly strong advantages in Code Quality & Complexity (92.5%) and Metric Relevance (83.887.5%). This demonstrates that evaluation skills, not just planning, drive quality gains. Against weaker baselines, advantages are even larger: B1 and B2 lack either iterative reasoning or trace access, yielding 90100% win-tie rates for EvalAgent. Evaluation success rate. Table 5b reports Eval@1. B1 achieves only 15.017.5%, confirming that single-pass generation is insufficient. B2 achieves the highest Eval@1 (45.060.0%) among baselines because source-code-only evaluation avoids trace parsing complexity. EvalAgent achieves 62.565.0%, substantially outperforming all baselines, including B4 (30.032.5%) which shares the same two-stage architecture but lacks evaluation skills. The stronger Sonnet backbone generally improves Eval@1 across methods. 9 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Efficiency. EvalAgent achieves favorable qualitycost trade-off (Table 5c). Compared to B4, which shares the same two-stage architecture, it uses 31% fewer tokens (2,095K vs. 3,024K for Sonnet) and 58% less time (4.18 vs. 10.00 min) while achieving higher quality. This demonstrates that evaluation skills reduce unnecessary exploration overhead. B1 is the most token-efficient but produces the lowest quality. 4.2. Findings We distill five empirical insights from our systematic design space exploration, identifying which components drive improvements and under what conditions. Ours vs. B1 Eval@1 by Requirement Approach Generic Specific Win-Tie Rate by Requirement Comparison Generic Specific 90.0% 97.4% vs. B2 100.0% 94.9% vs. B3 95.0% 81.6% vs. B4 97.4% 79.5% Finding 1: EvalAgent is robust under both generic and specific requirements. As Table 6 shows, EvalAgent maintains strong performance across both requirement types, achieving 62.565.0% Eval@1 regardless of specificity. Its advantage is especially pronounced under generic requirements (evaluate this agent without detailed specifications), where the system must infer appropriate metrics from agent behavior alone: 95.0% win-tie against B3 and 97.4% against B4. When requirements are well-specified, baselines partially close the gap (B3 rises from 20.0% to 32.5% Eval@1), but EvalAgent retains substantial lead (62.5% vs. 32.5%). This robustness across both conditions reflects the benefit of evaluation skills, which provide principled defaults when user guidance is minimal and complement domain-specific instructions when available. Tracebased evaluation (B3) achieves 73 77% win-tie rates over source-codeonly (B2), with the strongest advantage in Metric Relevance (80%). Traces enable metrics that assess what the agent actually didruntime tool calls, error recovery, and decision sequences invisible to static analysis. However, trace parsing introduces complexity that reduces Eval@1: B2 achieves 60.0% vs. B3s 35.0% with Sonnet. Part of this gap stems from the inherent difficulty of parsing traces, but B2s advantage is also inflated by evaluations that run on unit test stubs rather than real agent behavior. While trace-based evaluation imposes higher implementation overhead, it remains essential for capturing behavioral failures and decision-level errors that static code analysis cannot detect. Qualitative Analysis We compare EvalAgent against all four baselines on all 20 agents (generic requirements, Sonnet 4.5). Table 11 reports aggregate statistics; Appendix provides concrete examples including directory structures (Figure 3) and code comparisons (Figure 4). Insight 1: Without evaluation skills, baselines default to keyword matching instead of semantic evaluation. Keyword heuristicswhere the generated evaluation code scores agent outputs by counting regex or substring matches against hardcoded word listsappear in 3575% of baseline agents  (Table 11)  ; EvalAgent reduces this to 20%. For example, B4s singleagent_trip_crew scores itineraries via regex against 30 hardcoded action verbs, so the museum was closed scores identically to detailed visit plan (Figure 4). EvalAgent instead uses LLM-as-Judge (GEval) with structured criteria. Neither agentic reasoning, trace access, nor planning alone reliably shifts baselines from keyword matching to semantic evaluation; only evaluation skills do. Insight 2: Code complexity scales with autonomy level, but evaluation quality does not. EvalAgent produces the least code across all 20 agents (289 LOC), while baselines generate 2.46.6 more  (Table 11)  . Crucially, this extra code does not translate to better evaluation strategy. Failure Analysis We categorize all execution failures from EvalAgent across both backbones: Haiku 4.5 (15/40 failures, 62.5% Eval@1) and Sonnet 4.5 (14/40 failures, 65.0% Eval@1). Table 12 summarizes error categories. Model capability affects error types, not error rates. Sonnets failures concentrate on single systematic issue: DeepEval defaults to async_mode=True, but Sonnet implements only synchronous measure() without the required a_measure() coroutine. This accounts for 9/14 failures (64%); the generated code is structurally correct in every case and works when async_mode=False is set manually. Haikus failures are heterogeneous: Python API misuse (6 cases), timestamp string arithmetic (5 cases), plus import and syntax errors entirely absent from Sonnet. Haiku avoids the async trap because it generates simpler code that less frequently subclasses BaseMetric. 12 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Error Category Haiku Sonnet Description DeepEval async mode Type handling (timestamps) Python API misuse DeepEval API misuse DeepEval type mismatch Import / syntax errors Trace extraction logic 0 5 2 0 2 0 9 0 0 2 2 0 1 Missing a_measure() for async execution String timestamps not converted to int Invalid dict(os.stat()), Path.cti me(), etc. Wrong constructor / attribute access Dicts passed where strings expected Relative vs. absolute imports, indentation Empty actual_output from trace parsing Total failures 15 14 Table 12 Execution failure categorization. Haiku and Sonnet achieve similar pass rates but fail for disjoint reasons: Sonnet is dominated by DeepEval async mode (64%); Haiku by Python API misuse (40%) and timestamp handling (33%). Only DeepEval API misuse is shared (2 each). Failures are condition-dependent, not agent-dependent. For both backbones, most failing agents fail in only one requirement condition: Sonnet has 3 agents failing in both conditions vs. 8 in only one; Haiku shows the same 3-vs-9 split. Moreover, agents that fail in both conditions often fail for different reasonse.g., hilton_search_agent fails on type mismatch (generic) but async mode (specific). This suggests retry-based approaches may be effective, as re-generation would likely avoid the specific edge case. Implications. Fixing Sonnets async mode alone (a single parameter or 3-line wrapper) would raise Eval@1 from 65% to an estimated 87.5%the highest-leverage intervention. Haikus syntax and API errors (53% of its failures) indicate minimum capability threshold for evaluation code generation that smaller models may not meet. Context7 documentation retrieval is necessary but insufficient: framework-related errors account for 11/14 Sonnet and 2/15 Haiku failures despite up-to-date documentation access. 5. Related Work LLM judges. LLM-as-a-Judge (Fu et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023) is now standard alternative to human evaluation. Zheng et al. (2023) show that LLM judges can achieve over 80% agreement with humans; G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023) improves correlation via chain-of-thought prompting; ChatEval (Chan et al., 2023) employs multi-LLM debate to mimic human panel discussions. However, most LLM judges focus on single outputs and are therefore ill-suited to evaluate multi-step traces or agent behavior. Agentic judges. Agent-as-a-Judge (Zhuge et al., 2024) enables evaluators to execute code and browse documentation, but relies on manual task annotations and produces binary judgments. MAJEval (Chen et al., 2025) orchestrates multi-persona debates; DeepResearchEval (Wang et al., 2026) generates persona-driven tasks with adaptive fact-checking; CodeVisionary (Wang et al., 2025) distills multi-dimensional context from code requirements and produces fine-grained scores. These approaches use agents to directly judge target agents. In contrast, EvalAgent tackles different and harder challenge: It dynamically generates agent-specific evaluation pipelinescriteria, metrics, and executable codeto assess execution quality. 13 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Agent benchmarks. AgentBench (Liu et al., 2024) evaluates agents across environments including OS commands, databases, knowledge graphs, and web browsing. WebArena (Zhou et al., 2023), Mind2Web (Deng et al., 2023), and VisualWebArena (Koh et al., 2024) assess agents in web environments. SWE-Bench (Jimenez et al., 2024) focuses on resolving GitHub issues, and ToolLLM (Qin et al., 2024), ToolEmu (Ruan et al., 2023), and API-Bank (Li et al., 2023) benchmark tool-use capabilities. Other benchmarks target real computer tasks (Xie et al., 2024), web access (Mialon et al., 2023), workplace workflows (Xu et al., 2024), and scientific discovery (Chen et al., 2024). These outcome-only evaluations overlook agent dynamics. TRACE (Kim et al., 2025) evaluates agent trajectories using an evidence bank. TRAJECT-Bench (He et al., 2025) assesses fine-grained tool usage. These benchmarks assess agents against predefined objectives or reference trajectories. In contrast, EvalAgent provides trace-aware, reference-free evaluation across diverse agent types and domains. Agent evaluation frameworks. Several frameworks support agent evaluators through predefined, standardized metrics. DeepEval (Confident-AI, 2024) provides modular metrics with custom LLMas-judge support. RAGAS (Es et al., 2024) offers reference-free evaluation for retrieval-augmented generation. Langfuse (Rawert et al., 2023) models agents as trajectory loops supporting black-box, glass-box, and white-box evaluations. LangSmith (LangChain, 2023) enables evaluation via curated datasets and pluggable evaluators. These frameworks provide useful building blocks but require manual configuration per agentselecting metrics, writing instrumentation code, and designing evaluation logic. EvalAgent automates this full pipeline end-to-end. Meta-evaluation of judges. As LLM and agent judges become common, understanding their reliability is increasingly important. Prior work (Zheng et al., 2023) evaluates judges via correlation with human annotations, robustness to perturbations, and calibration studies. JudgeBench (Tan et al., 2024) benchmarks LLM judges on challenging response pairs across knowledge, reasoning, and coding tasks. Our meta-evaluation framework builds on pairwise comparison (Thurstone, 1927), construct validity (Cronbach and Meehl, 1955; Messick, 1995), and cross-model agreement inspired by generalizability theory (Brennan, 1992). Unlike prior work that evaluates the reliability of individual judgments, we evaluate the reliability of automated evaluation systems, including criteria generation, code synthesis, and plan-code alignment. 6. Conclusion As AI agents move from research prototypes to production systems, the gap between agent capabilities and evaluation capabilities widens. Developers can build sophisticated multi-step agents, but evaluating them remains largely manual, requiring domain expertise to design metrics, engineering effort to instrument code, and tedious inspection to diagnose failures. We introduced EvalAgent to close this gap. Its core insight is that general-purpose coding agents already have the capabilities for evaluation but lack evaluation-specific knowledge. EvalAgent encodes this missing knowledge as evaluation skillsprocedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamically retrieved API documentationthat compose into trace-based pipeline operating on runtime behavior (tool calls, error recovery, decision sequences) rather than source code alone. Our experiments across 20 diverse agents demonstrate that this approach achieves 84100% win-tie rates over baselines while maintaining 62.565.0% Eval@1, with 79.5% human expert preference. Three practical takeaways emerge for practitioners building agent evaluation: (1) incorporate execution traces whenever availablethe 7377% quality improvement over static analysis is substantial; (2) invest in evaluation skills rather than relying on general-purpose code generationstructured An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation guidance prevents the metric proliferation and plan-code drift that plague unconstrained approaches; and (3) ensure access to up-to-date API documentation, as the 45 percentage point Eval@1 gain from dynamic retrieval demonstrates this is not optional. Beyond the system itself, we contribute AgentEvalBench, the first benchmark for agent evaluation automation, and meta-evaluation framework validated by human experts. We hope these resources enable the research community to make progress on problem that will only grow more important as agents become more capable and more widely deployed. (i) Our benchmark covers 20 agents but may not represent all agent types (e.g., Limitations. embodied agents, multimodal agents). (ii) All experiments use the Claude model family; cross-model validation is needed to establish generalizability. (iii) Code executability of 62.565.0% means approximately one-third of generated evaluations require manual debugging. (iv) Meta-evaluation itself is inherently challenging and subjective: assessing whether an evaluation is good requires nuanced judgment about what matters for given agent, making this fundamentally difficult problem even for human experts. Future directions. Key directions include: (i) extending EvalAgent to support closed-loop iteration where evaluation feedback directly informs agent improvement; (ii) developing domain-specific evaluation skill libraries for different agent types (e.g., dialogue systems, embodied agents, coding assistants); (iii) advancing test data generation beyond simple synthesis to more sophisticated scenario construction; and (iv) establishing more generalizable trace format standardization. On the benchmark and methodology side, (v) expanding AgentEvalBench with additional agent types and (vi) developing more nuanced meta-evaluation methods remain important open challenges. EvalAgent Pipeline Details This section provides detailed descriptions of each phase in the EvalAgent pipeline, supplementing the overview in Section 2. A.1. Phase 1: Evaluation Planning The evaluation plan is documented in standardized Markdown format and includes: Agent Specification: Description of the agents purpose, capabilities, architecture, and technology stack. Evaluation Objectives: Clear, measurable goals aligned with user-specified requirements. Test Scenarios: High-level descriptions of interaction scenarios to be evaluated. Evaluation Metrics: 13 essential metrics with clear success criteria, expanding as needed. Technology Stack: Technical components for the evaluation (tracing libraries, evaluation frameworks, LLM providers, data formats). A.2. Phase 2: Test Case Generation Test cases are stored in JSON Lines (JSONL) format. Figure 1 shows an example structure. This phase is substitutable: alternative data generation approaches (e.g., Strands Evals generators) can be used as long as they emit test cases in the expected schema. Code 1 Example test case in JSONL format Each test case includes an identifier , scenario category , input query , description , and expected behavior for evaluation . { " test_id " : " general_info_001 " , " scenario " : " General Information Queries " , " query " : " What are the latest developments in AI ? " , " description " : " Tests agent ability to search and synthesize " , " expected_behavior " : " Agent should perform web search and provide factual answer " } A.3. Phase 3: Agent Instrumentation Figure 2 shows an example using Traceloop (Traceloop, 2026) for OTEL-compatible tracing. This lightweight instrumentation adds minimal overhead to the original codebase. A.4. Phase 4: Trace Collection and Processing Trace format. EvalAgent expects traces in OpenTelemetry-compatible JSON format. Each trace consists of spans with timestamps, event types, and structured payloads. Trace processing. trace processor converts OTEL JSONL into compact, per-trace JSON artifacts by filtering to agent-relevant spans (e.g., those carrying traceloop.*, gen_ai.*, or http.* attributes) and extracting minimal set of fields such as logical operation names, inputs/outputs, prompts/completions, tool metadata, and timing. A.5. Phase 5: Evaluation Code Generation The system generates three main components: 20 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Code 2 Example instrumentation code using Traceloop This minimal initialization enables OpenTelemetry - compatible tracing with traces exported to local collector . from traceloop . sdk import Traceloop Traceloop . init ( app_name = " { agent - name } " , disable_batch = True , api_endpoint = " http :// localhost :4318 " ) 1. Metric Implementations: Python classes implementing specific evaluation logic, including both deterministic checks (e.g., tool usage patterns) and LLM-based assessments (e.g., response quality, task completion). 2. Evaluation Orchestrator: Coordinates the evaluation pipeline: loading traces, applying metrics to each trace, and aggregating results. 3. Result Storage: Persists evaluation outcomes in JSON format for analysis and reporting. A.6. Phase 6: Reporting The evaluation report includes: Executive Summary: High-level results including test scale, success rate, key strengths, and critical issues. Results Analysis: Detailed breakdown of metric performance across test scenarios. Failure Analysis: Systematic analysis with root cause identification and specific evidence. Actionable Recommendations: Prioritized, evidence-based suggestions for improving the target agent. B. AgentEvalBench Dataset Details This section provides detailed descriptions of 20 agents in the AgentEvalBench dataset. B.1. Simple Complexity Agents AI Tic-Tac-Toe Agent. Domain: Game Playing. Framework: Agno. Description: game-playing agent that implements the classic Tic-Tac-Toe game using multi-agent architecture. The system demonstrates turn-based adversarial gameplay between two AI players. Multi-Agent: two agents (pl ayer_x and player_o) alternate turns in adversarial gameplay. Airbnb MCP Agent. Domain: Travel Assistant. Framework: MCP/Bedrock. Description: travel assistant agent that helps users search for and explore Airbnb listings. The agent leverages the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for tool integration with AWS Bedrock as the underlying LLM provider. Tools: 1 MCP tool for Airbnb search functionality. Browser MCP Agent. Domain: Web Browsing. Framework: MCP/Bedrock. Description: web browsing automation agent that can navigate websites, interact with page elements, and capture screenshots. The agent uses MCP tools to control browser actions programmatically. Tools: 4 MCP tools: browser_navigate (navigate to URLs), browser_click (click on page elements), browse r_evaluate (execute JavaScript), browser_take_screenshot (capture page screenshots). 21 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Career Assist. Domain: Career Planning. Framework: LangChain/LangGraph. Description: career planning assistant that helps users with various career-related tasks including job searching, resume creation, interview preparation, and finding learning resources. Tools: 5 LangGraph handlers: categ orize, handle_learning_resource, handle_resume_making, handle_interview_prepa ration, handle_job_search. Chat with PDF Agent. Domain: Document QA. Framework: Embedchain. Description: document question-answering agent that allows users to upload PDF documents and ask questions about their content. The agent uses vector embeddings for semantic search over document content. Tools: 2 functions: load_pdf (load and process PDF documents), ask_question (query the document content). Memory: ChromaDB vector database for document embeddings and retrieval. Chat with Research Papers. Domain: Research Assistant. Framework: Agno. Description: research assistant agent that helps users find and discuss academic papers from arXiv. The agent can search for papers based on topics and answer questions about research content. Tools: ArxivTools (search and retrieve papers from arXiv). Local News Agent. Domain: News Aggregation. Framework: Custom. Description: news aggregation agent that searches for, synthesizes, and summarizes local news. The agent employs multi-agent pipeline architecture for comprehensive news processing. Tools: DuckDuckGoSearchRe sults (web search for news articles). Multi-Agent: three specialized agents: search_agent (finds relevant news articles), synthesis_agent (combines information from multiple sources), summary _agent (creates concise summaries). MiniSWE Agent. Domain: Software Engineering. Framework: Custom. Description: minimal software engineering agent designed for code understanding and modification tasks. The agent represents lightweight approach to automated software engineering. Simple QA Search. Domain: Question Answering. Framework: LangGraph. Description: straightforward question-answering agent that combines web search with LLM reasoning to answer user queries. The agent demonstrates basic retrieval-augmented generation patterns. Tools: 1 search tool for web queries. Single Agent Trip Crew. Domain: Trip Planning. Framework: CrewAI. Description: trip planning agent built on the CrewAI framework that helps users plan travel itineraries by searching for destinations and scraping relevant travel information. Tools: 2 tools: SerperDevTool (search engine integration), ScrapeWebsiteTool (web content extraction). B.2. Medium Complexity Agents City Agent. Domain: Information Retrieval. Framework: LangGraph. Description: An information retrieval agent that provides city-related information including events, restaurant recommendations, search results, and weather data. The agent integrates multiple data sources for comprehensive city information. Tools: 4 tool classes: EventsDatabaseTool (query local events), RestaurantRec ommendationTool (find restaurants), SearchTool (general information search), WeatherTool (weather forecasts). CleverChatty Agent. Domain: Conversational AI. Framework: MCP. Description: conversational AI agent with persistent memory capabilities. The agent can remember information from past conversations and recall it in future interactions, enabling more contextual and personalized conversations. 22 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Tools: 3 MCP memory tools: remember (store information for later recall), recall (retrieve previously stored information), search_in_memory (search through stored memories). Memory: MCPMe moryClient with remember() and recall() methods for persistent conversation memory. Code Assistant. Domain: Code Generation. Framework: Strands. Description: code generation and review agent that can read projects, generate code, review existing code, write files, and execute code. The agent provides comprehensive software development assistance. Tools: 5 @tool decorated functions: project_reader (read and analyze project structure), code_generator (generate new code), code_reviewer (review code quality), code_writer_agent (write code to files), code_e xecute (execute code snippets). Data Warehouse Optimizer. Domain: Database Optimization. Framework: Strands. Description: database optimization agent that analyzes SQL queries, suggests optimizations, and validates query costs. The agent uses multi-agent workflow with specialized agents for different optimization tasks. Tools: 4 @tool decorated functions: get_query_execution_plan (analyze query execution), uggest_optimizations (recommend improvements), validate_query_cost (estimate query costs), calculator (perform calculations). Multi-Agent: three agents in LangGraph workflow: an alyzer_agent (analyzes query patterns), rewriter_agent (rewrites queries for optimization), alidator_agent (validates optimized queries). LlamaIndex Complex Agent. Domain: Financial Analysis. Framework: LlamaIndex. Description: financial analysis agent that uses multiple document indices for retrieval-augmented generation. The agent can analyze financial documents, calculate metrics, and compare companies. Memory: VectorSt oreIndex multi-index RAG system with chat memory buffer for maintaining conversation context. Medical Document Processor. Domain: Medical NLP. Framework: Strands. Description: medical document processing agent that extracts clinical information and links it to standard medical terminologies (ICD, RxNorm, SNOMED). The agent is designed for healthcare NLP applications. Tools: 8 tools: file_read, process_document, get_icd, get_rx, get_snomed, link_icd, link_rx, link_snomed. B.3. Hard Complexity Agents Adala Agent. Domain: Data Labeling. Framework: Custom-Adala. Description: sophisticated data labeling agent built on the Adala framework. The agent supports multiple labeling tasks and includes learning system that improves labeling quality over time through memory-based feedback. Tools: 9 skills: classification, summarization, qa, translation, entity_extraction, text_gen eration, rag, ontology_creator, multilabel_classification. Description: recommendation system agent that simulates user interactions to generate personalized recommendations. The agent uses memory with time and importance weighting for more accurate preference modeling. Tools: 3 tools: Arena simulation (simulate recommendation scenarios), Avatar (user preference modeling), Agent ask method (query the recommendation system). Memory: abstract_memory.py with add_ memory(), time_weighting(), importance_weighting(), and reflect() methods. 23 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Hilton Search Agent. Domain: Hotel Search. Framework: LangChain/LangGraph. Description: hotel search agent specialized for Hilton properties. The agent can search hotels, calculate costs, and help users utilize loyalty points for bookings. It maintains session-based memory for personalized search experiences. Tools: 3 @tool decorated functions: search_hotels (search Hilton properties), get_hotel_cost (calculate booking costs), utilize_points (apply loyalty points). Memory: ChromaDB vectorstore with session management for maintaining search context and user preferences across interactions. Network Switch Operator Agent. Domain: Network Management. Framework: Custom (Bedrock). Description: network management agent that monitors and operates network switches. The agent can retrieve device information, perform health checks, analyze network topology, and execute device reboots. Tools: 4 tool methods: get_device_summary (retrieve device information), reboot_dev ice (restart network devices), get_network_topology (map network structure), check_device _health (monitor device status). " } , { " text " : " Terrible " }] , " skill " : " classification " , " parameters " : { " labels " : [ " positive " , " negative " ]} } # Domain - specific format ( trip planning ) { " trip_request " : { " origin " : " Los Angeles , LAX " , " destination " : " Bali , Indonesia " , " duration " : " 10 days " } } B.5. Evaluation Requirements Examples Each agent has generic and specific evaluation requirements in YAML format. Figure 4 shows examples. C. Meta-Evaluation Dimension Rubrics We provide detailed comparative rubrics for each evaluation dimension used in meta-evaluation. The meta-evaluation is purely comparative with no numerical scores: for each dimension, the metaevaluator determines whether Wins, Wins, or Tie by directly comparing the two approaches. Winner determination. For each dimension, the overall winner is calculated by awarding points: the winner of dimension receives the full weight as points, while tie means both approaches receive half the weight. The approach with the higher total points wins overall. 24 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Code 4 Evaluation requirements examples Generic requirements test whether methods can infer metrics without guidance ; specific requirements provide domain - specific evaluation criteria . # Generic requirement " Given the agent codebase at ./ src and execution traces at ./ data / trace , create an evaluation plan " # Specific requirements # i _ u t _ c o " ... create an evaluation plan to evaluate correctness of extracted medical entities and diagnoses " # hilton_sear ch_ age nt " ... create an evaluation plan to evaluate how well hotel results match location , dates , and amenity requirements " # code_assistant " ... create an evaluation plan to evaluate correctness , efficiency , and best practices in generated code " C.1. User Requirement Fulfillment (Weight: 15%) This dimension assesses which evaluation more effectively addresses explicit user requirements. Outcome Comparative Criteria Wins Wins Tie Fulfills requirements opponent misses/addresses less directly; provides more focused implementation. Same as Wins with AB swapped. Both fulfill requirements equally, OR requirement is generic (e.g., create an evaluation plan) with nothing explicit to compare. Table 13 Rubric for User Requirement Fulfillment. Focused implementation beats exhaustive additions. C.2. Metric Relevance (Weight: 30%) This dimension evaluates which codebases metrics have better signal-to-noise ratio, receiving the highest weight due to its importance in evaluation quality. Signal refers to metrics that evaluate what matters for the evaluation goal; noise refers to metrics that do not add meaningful value (trivial, redundant, or distracting). Outcome Comparative Criteria Wins Wins Tie Better S/N ratiocovers essentials with less noise; more relevant metrics, less redundancy, clearer names. Same as Wins with AB swapped. Same S/N ratio, comparable redundancy/counts, or trade-offs balance out; similar metric clarity. Table 14 Rubric for Metric Relevance. More metrics better signal. Tie-breaker: metric name clarity. C.3. Code Quality & Complexity (Weight: 25%) This dimension evaluates which codebase has correct metric logic, cleaner implementation, and better organization. Win criteria are applied in priority order: (1) metric logic correctness, (2) code 25 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation organization, (3) clean implementation, (4) code readability. Outcome Comparative Criteria Wins Wins Tie Correct metric logic (opponent has 1 flaw); simpler organization (fewer files, shorter code) with equivalent functionality; no unused imports/over-engineering (opponent has some); clearer names. Same as Wins with AB swapped. Both have correct metric logic, similar file count/length, clean implementations, similar readability. Table 15 Rubric for Code Quality & Complexity. C.4. Plan Quality (Weight: 15%) This dimension assesses which evaluation plan is more coherent, complete, actionable, and efficiently documented. Win criteria are applied in priority order: (1) plan completeness, (2) definition completeness, (3) conciseness, (4) ease of understanding. Outcome Comparative Criteria Wins More complete structure (goals, metrics, methodology) while opponent misses essentials; equivalent comB Wins Tie pleteness with 50 fewer lines; more readable. Same as Wins with AB swapped. Both have all required components, complete definitions, similar length and readability. Table 16 Rubric for Plan Quality. Plans >1000 lines need justification; >1500 lines likely loses to shorter equivalent. C.5. Plan-Code Alignment (Weight: 15%) This dimension verifies which codebases implementation more faithfully follows its plan. Win criteria are applied in priority order: (1) metric alignment, (2) implementation mismatches, (3) missing implementations, (4) unplanned features. Outcome Comparative Criteria Wins Wins Tie All metrics aligned (opponent has 1 misaligned); zero implementation mismatches (opponent has 1); all planned features implemented (opponent missing 1); zero unplanned features (opponent has 1). Same as Wins with AB swapped. Both have all metrics aligned, no mismatches, missing/unplanned features within 1 each. Table 17 Rubric for Plan-Code Alignment. D. Prompt Specifications This section provides the key prompts used in EvalAgent. Due to space constraints, we present structured summaries with key components highlighted. Full prompt templates are available at https://github.com/awslabs/Agent-EvalKit. D.1. Baseline Approaches Table 18 summarizes the baseline approaches compared against EvalAgent. 26 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Approach Stages Tool Access Context Delivery LLM-SingleTurn (B1) Agent-SourceCode (B2) Agent-OneStage (B3) Agent-TwoStage (B4) EvalAgent (Ours) 1 1 1 2 2 None Full Full Full Full Embedded in prompt Filesystem (no traces) Filesystem Filesystem Filesystem + templates Table 18 Baseline approaches for evaluation code generation. B1: LLM Single-Turn Baseline Context Delivery: All context embedded directly in prompt (no tool access) Prompt Structure: # Agent Source Code: ## File: {path} {file_contents for all source files} # EXECUTION TRACES {trace contents} # EVALUATION TASK {evaluation_requirements} ## Output Format (STRICT) ### File: <filename>.py Note: Pure text completion with strict output format. No iterative exploration. B2: Agent Source-Code Baseline Context: Agent source code via filesystem, no execution traces Prompt: Given the agent codebase, directly implement evaluation code in Python. Tool Access: Full agent tools (file operations, search, code editing, shell commands) B3: Agent One-Stage Baseline Context: Agent source code + execution traces via filesystem Prompt: Given the agent codebase and execution traces, directly implement evaluation code in Python. Tool Access: Full agent tools (file operations, search, code editing, shell commands) Note: Single-stage generation without intermediate planning. 27 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation B4: Agent Two-Stage Baseline Context: Agent source code + execution traces via filesystem Stage 1 (Plan) Prompt: Given the agent codebase and execution traces, create an evaluation plan. Stage 2 (Code) Prompt: Given the evaluation plan, implement evaluation code. Tool Access: Full agent tools for both stages Note: Two-stage without structured templates or reference implementations. D.2. EvalAgent Prompts

sections.title: Planning Prompt
sections.content: Role: Evaluation Strategy Designer Input: Agent source code, execution traces (OTEL format), user requirements Workflow: 1. Analyze agent architecture, capabilities, and behavior patterns 2. Review execution traces to understand runtime behavior 3. Identify 24 key metrics capturing core agent behaviors 4. Design test scenarios exercising critical functionality Focus on actionable content. Each metric must measure distinct behavioral aspect. Output: Structured evaluation plan with agent analysis, evaluation goals, metrics with scoring rubrics, test scenarios, and implementation notes.

sections.title: Code Generation Prompt
sections.content: Role: Evaluation Code Implementer Input: Evaluation plan, OTEL traces Workflow: 1. Implement metrics from evaluation plan 2. Create metric classes with extraction functions for raw OTEL data 3. Build main entry point for evaluation pipeline 4. Code review and fix critical issues 5. Update dependencies Key Principles: Create minimal working version first. Validate library APIs before implementation. Avoid over-engineering; follow plan exactly. Code Quality: Target 200400 LOC, 24 files, no unnecessary abstractions. Output: Metrics implementation, evaluation runner, requirements file D.3. Evaluation Skills Examples EvalAgents evaluation skills comprise procedural instructions, reusable code and templates, and dynamic resources. Together, they standardize the end-to-end workflow, reduce run-to-run variance, and improve Eval@1. 28 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation Evaluation Code Generation Context7 Validation Requirement ### Context7 Validation **MANDATORY**: Before implementing functionality from any publicly available evaluation library, validate its current API usage with Context7 MCP: - "Whats the current DeepEval LLMTestCase constructor signature?" - "Show me the latest DeepEval BaseMetric implementation patterns" - "What are the current built-in metrics available in DeepEval?" This ensures production-ready code that follows current best practices and avoids deprecated patterns. Reusable recipes: plan/report templates. Templates provide structured scaffolding for evaluation artifacts. The plan template defines sections for agent analysis, metrics specification, and progress tracking. The report template provides structured scaffolding for evaluation reports, ensuring consistent documentation of results, analysis, and recommendations. Plan Template Evaluation Metrics Section ## 3. Evaluation Metrics <!-- ACTION REQUIRED: If no specific user requirements are provided, use minimal number of metrics (1-2 metrics) focusing on the most critical aspects of agent performance. --> ### [Metric Name 1] - **Evaluation Area:** [Final response quality/tool call accuracy/...] - **Description:** [What is measured and why] - **Method:** [Code-based LLM-as-Judge] Excerpt: eval-report-template.md Executive Summary Section # Agent Evaluation Report for [AGENT NAME] ## Executive Summary - **Test Scale**: [N] test cases - **Success Rate**: [XX.X%] - **Status**: [Excellent/Good/Poor] - **Strengths**: [Specific capability] [Performance highlight] - **Critical Issues**: [Blocking issue + impact] [Bottleneck] - **Action Priority**: [Critical fixes] [Improvements] ## Agent Failure Analysis ### Issue 1 - [Priority Level] - **Issue**: [Clear problem statement with evaluation metrics] - **Root Cause**: [Technical analysis path/to/file.py:START-END] - **Priority Fixes**: - P1 [Fix name]: [Solution] Expected gain: [Metric +X] Reusable recipes: code patterns. Code patterns provide implementation scaffolding for trace extraction and metrics. The patterns demonstrate OTEL JSONL parsing and DeepEval integration. 29 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation

sections.title: Trace Extraction Pattern
sections.content: def get_span_attribute(span: Dict, key: str) -> Optional[str]: """Extract attribute value from OTEL span attributes array.""" for attr in span.get("attributes", []): if attr.get("key") == key: value = attr.get("value", {}) return (value.get("stringValue") or value.get("intValue") or value.get("boolValue") or value.get("doubleValue")) return None def load_trace_to_test_case(file_path: str) -> LLMTestCase: """Convert OTEL JSONL trace file to DeepEval test case.""" spans = load_spans_from_jsonl(file_path) return LLMTestCase( input=extract_user_input(spans), actual_output=extract_final_response(spans) )

sections.title: Custom Metric Pattern
sections.content: class TaskCompletionMetric(BaseMetric): """Custom metric using G-Eval for task completion assessment.""" def __init__(self, threshold: float = 0.7): self.threshold = threshold self.geval = GEval( name="Task Completion", criteria="Evaluate whether the agent successfully completed the requested task based on input and output.", evaluation_params=[LLMTestCaseParams.INPUT, LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT], model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022" ) def measure(self, test_case: LLMTestCase) -> float: self.geval.measure(test_case) self.score = self.geval.score return self.score An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation D.4. Meta-Evaluator Comparison Prompt Meta-Evaluator Prompt Role: Comparative Meta-Evaluator Task: Compare evaluation system vs. to determine which is superior Evaluation Dimensions (weighted): 1. User Requirement Fulfillment (15%) explicit requirement coverage 2. Metric Relevance (30%) signal-to-noise ratio of metrics 3. Code Quality & Complexity (25%) correctness, organization 4. Plan Quality (15%) coherence, completeness, conciseness 5. Plan-Code Alignment (15%) faithfulness to plan Anti-Length Bias: Conciseness is virtue. Quality over quantity (fewer focused metrics preferred). Practitioner perspective: Which would developer maintain? Red flags: 20+ metrics, code 2 necessary length, plan >1500 lines. Workflow: Navigate to each approachs directory Read plans and code Determine winner per dimension (A/B/Tie) Calculate weighted score Provide evidence-based justification Output: Per-dimension winners with justification, points calculation (out of 1.00), overall winner, key differentiating factors E. Human Annotation Study E.1. Study Design Three expert annotators (3 years ML/AI engineering experience, agent development expertise, no prior involvement in EvalAgent) independently evaluated 40 agent-evaluation pairs across 5 dimensions (200 judgments total). Annotators were blind to system identity with randomized A/B assignment. Each completed 2-hour training session with 5 practice annotations and calibration round. Average annotation time was 68 minutes per case. E.2. Annotation Instructions The following instructions were provided to annotators: 31 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation

Meta-Evaluation Consistency Analysis This section provides detailed consistency analysis for the meta-evaluator, examining both cross-model agreement and run-to-run stability. H.1. Cross-Model Consistency Beyond human alignment, we assess meta-evaluator reliability through cross-model consistency: whether different LLM models serving as meta-evaluators reach the same conclusions when evaluating 34 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation identical evaluation pairs. We ran the complete meta-evaluation pipeline using two distinct models: Claude Opus 4.5 and Claude Sonnet 4.5. Both models evaluated the same pairwise comparisons across four baseline configurations (LLM-Singleturn (B1), Agent-Sourcecode (B2), Agent-Onestage (B3), and Agent-Twostage (B4) versus EvalAgent). Agreement reports the percentage of individual evaluations on which both meta-evaluators selected the same outcome. Results aggregated across two evaluator LLMs (Haiku 4.5, Sonnet 4.5), two requirement types (generic, specific), and three independent runs. As shown in Table 24, both models reach qualitatively identical conclusions about relative evaluation quality: EvalAgent achieves win-tie rates above 80% against every baseline under both metaevaluators, with inter-model gaps ranging from 0.6 to 8.8 percentage points. The overall cross-model agreement rate of 86.6% confirms that the two meta-evaluators concur on the vast majority of individual judgments. Notably, the ranking of baselines by difficulty is preserved across modelsboth identify B1 and B2 as the easiest comparisons and B3 and B4 as the most competitiveindicating that the meta-evaluators capture the same underlying quality gradient. This cross-model consistency provides complementary evidence to human validation. While human studies establish alignment with expert judgment, cross-model agreement demonstrates that metaevaluation outcomes are robust to the choice of underlying LLM. H.2. Run-to-Run Consistency third signal of meta-evaluator reliability is run-to-run consistency: whether the same meta-evaluator model produces consistent judgments across independent runs. High consistency indicates that evaluations reflect stable reasoning rather than stochastic artifacts of sampling. We executed the complete meta-evaluation pipeline three times using Claude Opus 4.5 across all 40 comparison configurations (20 agents 2 evaluation requirement types). Across the evaluations, all three runs agreed on 76.3% of cases and the average pairwise agreement was 84.2%. Three-way agreement indicates all three runs selected the same winner; pairwise average reports the mean agreement across all three run pairs. Table 25 presents three-way and pairwise agreement rates stratified by evaluation dimension, averaged across all 40 configurations. Code Quality & Complexity exhibits the highest consistency (84.6% 35 An Empirical Study of Automating Agent Evaluation three-way, 89.7% pairwise), likely because code-level properties offer more concrete, less ambiguous evidence for comparison. Metric Relevance and User Requirement Fulfillment show moderately lower but still strong agreement, consistent with the greater interpretive latitude these dimensions afford. Overall, the results demonstrate that the meta-evaluator produces stable judgments across independent runs: 76.3% three-way agreement rate across 40 configurations substantially exceeds the 33.3% baseline expected from random three-way choices among three outcomes (A wins, wins, tie). The accompanying 84.2% pairwise average further confirms that disagreements, when they occur, are typically confined to single divergent run rather than reflecting systematic instability. These findings support the conclusion that our meta-evaluation methodology yields reproducible judgments suitable for comparing evaluation systems at scale. I.
